label: Definition 2.5.1 (Primitive Root).
title: Primitive Root
contents: A primitive root modulo an integer $n$ is an element of $(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$ of order $\varphi(n)$.

bodylist.description: A primitive root modulo an integer $n$ is an element of $(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$ of order $\varphi(n)$.

label: Proposition 2.5.3
title: Root Bound
contents: **Proposition 2.5.3** (Root Bound).
*Let $f \in k[x]$ be a nonzero polynomial over a field $k$. Then there are at most $\deg(f)$ elements $\alpha \in k$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.*

bodylist.description:
**Proposition 2.5.3** (Root Bound).

*Let $f \in k[x]$ be a nonzero polynomial over a field $k$.
Then there are at most $\deg(f)$ elements $\alpha \in k$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.*

contents: *Proof.* We prove the proposition by induction on $\deg(f)$. The cases in which $\deg(f) \leq 1$ are clear. Write $f = a_n x^n + \cdots + a_1 x + a_0$. If $f(\alpha) = 0$, then

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= f(x) - f(\alpha) \\ &= a_n(x^n - \alpha^n) + \cdots + a_1(x - \alpha) + a_0(1 - 1) \\ &= (x - \alpha)(a_n(x^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha^{n-1}) + \cdots + a_2(x + \alpha) + a_1) \\ &= (x - \alpha)g(x), \end{aligned}$$

for some polynomial $g(x) \in k[x]$. Next, suppose that $f(\beta) = 0$ with $\beta \neq \alpha$. Then $(\beta - \alpha)g(\beta) = 0$, so, since $\beta - \alpha \neq 0$ and $k$ is a field, we have $g(\beta) = 0$. By our inductive hypothesis, $g$ has at most $n - 1$ roots, so there are at most $n - 1$ possibilities for $\beta$. It follows that $f$ has at most $n$ roots. $\square$

bodylist.description: We prove the proposition by induction on $\deg(f)$.
The cases in which $\deg(f) \leq 1$ are clear.
Write $f = a_n x^n + \cdots + a_1 x + a_0$.

If $f(\alpha) = 0$, then

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= f(x) - f(\alpha) \\ &= a_n(x^n - \alpha^n) + \cdots + a_1(x - \alpha) + a_0(1 - 1) \\ &= (x - \alpha)(a_n(x^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha^{n-1}) + \cdots + a_2(x + \alpha) + a_1) \\ &= (x - \alpha)g(x), \end{aligned}$$

for some polynomial $g(x) \in k[x]$.
Next, suppose that $f(\beta) = 0$ with $\beta \neq \alpha$.
Then $(\beta - \alpha)g(\beta) = 0$, so, since $\beta - \alpha \neq 0$ and $k$ is a field, we have $g(\beta) = 0$.
By our inductive hypothesis, $g$ has at most $n - 1$ roots, so there are at most $n - 1$ possibilities for $\beta$.
It follows that $f$ has at most $n$ roots. $\square$

label: Proposition 2.5.5
title: Roots of x^d - 1 in Z/pZ
contents: **Proposition 2.5.5.** *Let $p$ be a prime number and let $d$ be a divisor of $p - 1$. Then $f = x^d - 1 \in (\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})[x]$ has exactly $d$ roots in $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$.*

bodylist.description: Let $p$ be a prime number and let $d$ be a divisor of $p - 1$.
Then $f = x^d - 1 \in (\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})[x]$ has exactly $d$ roots in $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$.

contents: *Proof.* Let $e = (p - 1)/d$. We have

where $g \in (\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})[x]$ and $\deg(g) = de - d = p - 1 - d$. Theorem 2.1.20 implies that $x^{p-1} - 1$ has exactly $p - 1$ roots in $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$, since every nonzero element of $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$ is a root! By Proposition 2.5.3, $g$ has *at most* $p - 1 - d$ roots and $x^d - 1$ has at most $d$ roots. Since a root of $(x^d - 1)g(x)$ is a root of either $x^d - 1$ or $g(x)$ and $x^{p-1} - 1$ has $p - 1$ roots, $g$ must have exactly $p - 1 - d$ roots and $x^d - 1$ must have exactly $d$ roots, as claimed. $\square$

bodylist.description: *Proof.* Let $e = (p - 1)/d$. We have

$$\begin{aligned} x^{p-1} - 1 &= (x^d)^e - 1 \\ &= (x^d - 1)((x^d)^{e-1} + (x^d)^{e-2} + \cdots + 1) \\ &= (x^d - 1)g(x), \end{aligned}$$

where $g \in (\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})[x]$ and $\deg(g) = de - d = p - 1 - d$.
Theorem 2.1.20 implies that $x^{p-1} - 1$ has exactly $p - 1$ roots in $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$, since every nonzero element of $\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}$ is a root!
By Proposition 2.5.3, $g$ has *at most* $p - 1 - d$ roots and $x^d - 1$ has at most $d$ roots.

Since a root of $(x^d - 1)g(x)$ is a root of either $x^d - 1$ or $g(x)$ and $x^{p-1} - 1$ has $p - 1$ roots, $g$ must have exactly $p - 1 - d$ roots and $x^d - 1$ must have exactly $d$ roots, as claimed.
$\square$

label: Lemma 2.5.7
title: Order of Product of Commuting Elements with Coprime Orders
contents: **Lemma 2.5.7.** Suppose $a, b \in (\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$ have orders $r$ and $s$, respectively, and that $\gcd(r, s) = 1$. Then $ab$ has order $rs$.

bodylist.description: Suppose $a, b \in (\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$ have orders $r$ and $s$, respectively, and that $\gcd(r, s) = 1$.
Then $ab$ has order $rs$.

contents: *Proof.* This is a general fact about commuting elements of any group; our proof only uses that $ab = ba$ and nothing special about $(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$. Since
the order of $ab$ is a divisor of $rs$. Write this divisor as $r_1s_1$ where $r_1 \mid r$ and $s_1 \mid s$. Raise both sides of the equation
to the power $r_2 = r/r_1$ to obtain
so $s \mid r_1r_2s_1$. Since $\gcd(s, r_1r_2) = \gcd(s, r) = 1$, it follows that $s = s_1$. Similarly $r = r_1$, so the order of $ab$ is $rs$. $\square$

bodylist.description: *Proof.* This is a general fact about commuting elements of any group; our proof only uses that $ab = ba$ and nothing special about $(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})^*$. Since
the order of $ab$ is a divisor of $rs$.
Write this divisor as $r_1s_1$ where $r_1 \mid r$ and $s_1 \mid s$.

Raise both sides of the equation

$$ a^{r_1s_1}b^{r_1s_1} = (ab)^{r_1s_1} = 1 $$

to the power $r_2 = r/r_1$ to obtain

$$ a^{r_1r_2s_1}b^{r_1r_2s_1} = 1. $$
so $s \mid r_1r_2s_1$.
Since $\gcd(s, r_1r_2) = \gcd(s, r) = 1$, it follows that $s = s_1$.
Similarly $r = r_1$, so the order of $ab$ is $rs$. $\square$

label: Theorem 2.5.8 (Primitive Roots)
title: Primitive Roots
contents: **Theorem 2.5.8 (Primitive Roots).** There is a primitive root modulo any prime $p$. In particular, the group $(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})^*$ is cyclic.

bodylist.description: **Theorem 2.5.8 (Primitive Roots).** There is a primitive root modulo any prime $p$. In particular, the group $(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})^*$ is cyclic.

contents: *Proof.* The theorem is true if $p = 2$, since 1 is a primitive root, so we may assume $p > 2$. Write $p - 1$ as a product of distinct prime powers $q_i^{n_i}$:
By Proposition 2.5.5, the polynomial $x^{q_i^{n_i}} - 1$ has exactly $q_i^{n_i}$ roots, and the polynomial $x^{q_i^{n_i-1}} - 1$ has exactly $q_i^{n_i-1}$ roots. There are $q_i^{n_i} - q_i^{n_i-1} =$

bodylist.description: *Proof.* The theorem is true if $p = 2$, since 1 is a primitive root, so we may assume $p > 2$.

Write $p - 1$ as a product of distinct prime powers $q_i^{n_i}$:

$$ p - 1 = q_1^{n_1}q_2^{n_2}\cdots q_r^{n_r}. $$
By Proposition 2.5.5, the polynomial $x^{q_i^{n_i}} - 1$ has exactly $q_i^{n_i}$ roots, and the polynomial $x^{q_i^{n_i-1}} - 1$ has exactly $q_i^{n_i-1}$ roots.
